 002: #
 003: # Cython bridge: libpq binary wire format → numpy arrays → pandas DataFrame
 004: #
 005: # Compile with:   python build.py build_ext --inplace
 006: #
 007: # What happens here that doesn't happen in normal drivers:
 008: #   - We call PQexecParams with resultFormat=1 (binary), so Postgres sends
 009: #     raw typed bytes instead of text representations.
 010: #   - We pre-allocate numpy arrays of the correct dtype BEFORE iterating rows.
 011: #   - PQgetvalue() returns a char* directly into libpq's result buffer.
 012: #   - We byte-swap and write straight into arr.data — no Python objects on
 013: #     the hot path for numeric types.
 014: #   - The GIL is released for the entire extraction loop per numeric column.
 015: 
+016: import numpy as np
  __pyx_t_1 = __Pyx_Import(__pyx_mstate_global->__pyx_n_u_numpy, 0, 0, NULL, 0); if (unlikely(!__pyx_t_1)) __PYX_ERR(0, 16, __pyx_L1_error)
  __pyx_t_2 = __pyx_t_1;
  __Pyx_GOTREF(__pyx_t_2);
  if (PyDict_SetItem(__pyx_mstate_global->__pyx_d, __pyx_mstate_global->__pyx_n_u_np, __pyx_t_2) < (0)) __PYX_ERR(0, 16, __pyx_L1_error)
  __Pyx_DECREF(__pyx_t_2); __pyx_t_2 = 0;

 211: @cython.wraparound(False)
 212: cdef cnp.ndarray extract_timestamp(PGresult* res, int col, int nrows):
 213:     """
 214:     Postgres TIMESTAMP binary = int64 microseconds since 2000-01-01.
 215:     We store as int64 and let pandas convert to datetime64[us] afterward.
 216:     """
